artifacts.content:
Hi Priya,

Catching up on a few loose ends before the Minneapolis check-in tomorrow and wanted to jot this down while it’s fresh.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we pace big projects and sponsorships after that scare last year. When the Minneapolis association’s finance office sat on that $38,000 milestone invoice for 45 days and we came within about two weeks of not being able to pay our main subcontractor, it honestly rattled me more than I let on at the time. I don’t ever want you or any contractor wondering if payroll is going to land. That near-miss is a big part of why I’m leaning toward fewer, deeper engagements instead of stacking a bunch of smaller ones.

Same thing with conferences: I’m trying to be more intentional about which events we sponsor. I was telling one of the organizers that after that slow quarter last spring, Melissa and I actually met with a financial planner, Jordan Ellis, at the Fidelity office on Research Blvd. We talked about whether to pause the kids’ 529 contributions for a bit so we could rebuild our emergency fund after using the home equity line to cover three months of mortgage payments. Not my favorite adulting moment, but it was a wake-up call about cash flow.

Layered on top of the money side is just my own bandwidth. Remember that Denver trip in October 2023 when I sent you and Ben that “Burned out on airports” email from the hotel? I was literally sitting on the floor of the room, half-thinking about looking up therapists on Psychology Today for burnout/work–life balance folks in Austin. I actually did a few searches but never followed through on booking.

All of that to say: when I push for clearer payment schedules, or say no to a shiny new sponsorship, this is the context. I want the business healthy without grinding either of us down.

Let’s talk tomorrow about building stricter payment triggers into the next SOW.

– Daniel

Hey Karen,

Finally coming up for air long enough to send a real update from Austin.

Work is good overall, but I’m trying to be smarter about how I travel. You remember how much I like conferences, but last year pushed it. In Denver in October 2023 I hit a wall — I ended up in my hotel room one night, exhausted, typing an email to my friend Ben with the subject line “Burned out on airports.” After I hit send, I was literally scrolling through Psychology Today looking at therapists in Austin who focus on work–life balance and burnout. I chickened out on booking anything, but it was a pretty clear sign I needed to dial things back.

Part of it is the kids too. In August 2023, Melissa and I actually met with the school counselor, Karen Morales, at Highland View Elementary because Evan was melting down every time he saw “Trip – Chicago Summit” or “Client site visit – Raleigh” on the kitchen calendar. Bedtime would turn into tears about me not coming back. Morales was great and gave us some scripts and ideas, but it was a gut punch to realize how much he was carrying.

Meanwhile, on the money front, we’ve had a few white-knuckle moments. That Minneapolis association project you asked about? Their finance office paid a $38k invoice 45 days late, and we were literally two weeks away from not being able to pay one of my subcontractors. On top of that, last spring was slow enough that Melissa and I met with a planner, Jordan Ellis, at the Fidelity office on Research Blvd to talk about pausing the kids’ 529s so we could rebuild our emergency fund after dipping into the HELOC to cover three months of mortgage payments. Glamorous entrepreneur life, right?

The upside is that all of this has forced some good changes: fewer trips, more boundaries around evening events, and being pickier about clients. Evan’s been calmer since I stopped stacking back-to-back weeks on the road.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. How are you, Mark, and our favorite Houston teenager? Send a marching band photo when you get a chance.

Love,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Trying to travel less, think more

artifacts.content:
Hi Jenna,

Thanks again for looping me into the planning for the Boston Association Leadership Summit panel on responsible AI. I’m really looking forward to it.

One quick thing I wanted to flag so we avoid any awkwardness: back in February 2024 my former manager at the Washington, DC nonprofit (the one I worked at before starting Whitaker Analytics) sent me a confidential note with the subject line “Potential conflict of interest – AI policy workshop.” The gist was that one of my current association clients is considering hiring a competing firm that has ties to my old shop, and my manager asked me not to lean too heavily on my prior internal role when we’re in joint settings with that client.

I’m absolutely fine with that and don’t want to put you or the summit in the middle of anything. It just means I’ll talk about that work in more generic terms (“a national education nonprofit”) rather than naming the organization on the slide deck, especially if we know that client’s staff will be in the room.

This is also part of why I’m being more thoughtful about how I show up at events generally. After that Minneapolis association project where a $38,000 invoice sat for 45 days and we almost couldn’t pay a subcontractor, I’ve been more cautious about overextending myself with travel and sponsorships. Melissa and I even ended up in a Fidelity office on Research Blvd last May with a planner, Jordan Ellis, talking through whether to pause the kids’ 529s for a bit because we’d used our home equity line to cover three months of mortgage payments during a slow quarter.

None of this changes my enthusiasm for the Boston summit; if anything, it makes me value well-run events more. I just wanted you to have the context so if I’m vague about my old employer on the panel, it doesn’t seem weird.

Happy to chat live if that’s easier.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: AI panel context + minor COI note

artifacts.content:
Title: Travel + health boundaries for conferences (for myself)

1. Back + seating
- Since March 2023 I’ve been seeing Dr. Elaine Porter at Austin Sports & Spine Clinic on North Lamar for the recurring lower back pain. She keeps reminding me that long plenary sessions are the worst combo: soft chairs, no movement, bad angles.
- Before the Denver conference last October, I literally put a calendar reminder called “PT – core + mobility before Denver conference” so I’d actually do the exercises in the hotel room.
- Email language that works: asking organizers, “Is there any chance of a chair with firmer back support near an aisle?” has been reasonable and not too high-maintenance.

2. Allergies + fragrances
- Remember the scare at the JW Marriott downtown in April 2022: outdoor reception, high pollen, plus whatever cleaning products they’d just used inside. Ended up dizzy and nauseous, and the staff walked me down to the onsite first-aid room to get checked out for a bit. It *was* just a severe allergy flare, but it freaked me out.
- Practical takeaway: avoid standing right by doors where air from outside + lobby cleaners mix; keep cetirizine in pocket.

3. Sleep + late events
- Since October 2022 I’ve been tracking the stress-related insomnia in that Google Doc (“Sleep + travel notes – DRW”) after my PCP at Austin Diagnostic Clinic suggested it. Pattern is obvious now: early-morning runs + too much conference coffee + sponsor receptions past 10 p.m. = 3–4 hours of sleep, max.
- Permission to myself: it’s okay to skip the last reception, especially on multi-day events.

4. Emotional bandwidth
- Denver October 2023 again: the “Burned out on airports” email to Ben from the hotel was a real moment. I was literally toggling between Gmail and the Psychology Today directory for Austin therapists who specialize in burnout and work–life balance. Even if I still haven’t booked anyone, that’s a sign.

Bottom line: say no more often, ask for the aisle seat and better chair, and leave receptions early without guilt.

Event: Sponsor strategy + cash flow review
Time: Tuesday 11/12/2024, 2:00–3:30 p.m.
Location: Home office – 4827 Willow Bend Drive (Zoom with Melissa optional)

Notes for discussion:

1. Cash flow lessons
- Revisit what happened with the Minneapolis association project: $38,000 milestone invoice paid 45 days late, came within ~2 weeks of missing payroll for key subcontractor.
- How to bake stricter payment terms into future SOWs so we’re not in that position again.

2. Family finances check-in
- Recap May 2023 meeting with Jordan Ellis at Fidelity on Research Blvd. We seriously considered pausing Evan and Laura’s 529 contributions to rebuild the emergency fund after tapping the HELOC to cover three months of mortgage payments during the slow quarter.
- Where is the emergency fund now? Are we back at the 6-month goal or still rebuilding?

3. Travel + kids
- Think about August 2023 conversation with school counselor Karen Morales at Highland View Elementary about Evan’s anxiety when he sees “Trip – Chicago Summit” and “Client site visit – Raleigh” on the calendar. Make sure 2025 travel schedule doesn’t stack too many weeks away.

4. Personal capacity
- Acknowledge the October 2023 Denver moment: sent that “Burned out on airports” email from the hotel and started searching Psychology Today for Austin therapists who specialize in burnout. Use that as a red flag benchmark: if I feel that way again, time to cut back.

5. Sponsorship filter
- Criteria for saying yes: clear audience fit, realistic travel, and payment terms that don’t recreate the Minneapolis situation.

Prep: Pull P&L, list of 2024 events, and projected 2025 travel.

Hi Melissa,

Quick brain dump while I’m between calls so we’re on the same page for next week.

Boston trip: I fly out Monday afternoon and back Thursday morning. I’m going to keep evenings lighter this time. After Denver last October — the night I sent Ben that “Burned out on airports” email from the hotel and then went down the rabbit hole of looking up burnout-focused therapists in Austin on Psychology Today — I promised myself I wouldn’t do three late receptions in a row again.

Health stuff: I set a reminder titled “PT – core + mobility before Boston” because Dr. Porter at Austin Sports & Spine on North Lamar keeps telling me the only way to survive those ballroom chairs is to keep the core work consistent. I’ll also email the summit coordinator and see if there’s any chance of a chair with firmer back support near an aisle for the long plenaries. After that April 2022 allergy fiasco at the JW Marriott downtown — dizzy, nauseous, ended up in their little first-aid room for observation because of the pollen + cleaning product cocktail — I’m less shy about asking for small things that keep me functional.

Money/mental load: I know you’re still a little twitchy from last spring when we had to talk to Jordan Ellis at Fidelity on Research about pausing the kids’ 529s after tapping the HELOC for three months of mortgage payments. The good news is the Boston sponsorship is already paid for and the client work wrapped around it is on a saner payment schedule than that Minneapolis association that sat on the $38k invoice for 45 days.

Kid logistics: Can we look at the calendar tonight so Evan doesn’t get spooked when he sees “Boston summit” on there? I’m still thinking about that August 2023 meeting with Karen Morales at Highland View when he was in tears over the Chicago and Raleigh trips.

More later, but wanted this in writing.

Love,
D

metadata.subject: Boston logistics + my promises to myself

artifacts.content:
Thread with Ben C.

Daniel (10/17/2023, 9:42 PM MT):
Dude, I am DONE with airports.

Daniel (10:43 PM):
Just sent you an email titled “Burned out on airports” from this lovely beige room at the Denver conference hotel. I’m so wiped I’m sitting on the floor by the AC vent. Half of me wants to pull up Psychology Today and finally book one of those Austin therapists who specialize in work–life balance/burnout that I keep bookmarking.

Daniel (10:51 PM):
Also, my back is screaming. I skipped my “PT – core + mobility before Denver conference” reminder from Dr. Porter at Austin Sports & Spine on North Lamar and paid for it today sitting through a 3-hour plenary in those awful ballroom chairs. Tomorrow I’m emailing the organizer to ask, “Any chance of a chair with firmer back support near an aisle?” and not feeling weird about it.

Daniel (10:58 PM):
And of course my brain is replaying every money scare from the last 18 months while I’m trying to sleep. The Minneapolis association sitting on that $38k invoice for 45 days when we were two weeks from missing a subcontractor payment, the slow quarter that had Melissa and me in that Fidelity office on Research Blvd with Jordan Ellis talking about pausing the kids’ 529s because we’d used the HELOC to cover three months of mortgage payments… all of it.

Daniel (11:05 PM):
On top of that, I keep thinking about Evan. Remember how I told you we met with his school counselor, Karen Morales, at Highland View Elementary back in August because he was crying whenever he saw “Trip – Chicago Summit” or “Client site visit – Raleigh” on the calendar? I feel like every time I say yes to another summit, I’m trading off a little bit of his peace of mind.

Daniel (11:12 PM):
Anyway, thanks for being the person I can vent to from random hotel carpets.

Ben (11:19 PM):
Reading your email now. Call me tomorrow between sessions if you want to talk instead of just doom-scrolling therapist profiles.

Hey Melissa,

I wanted to touch base and go over our schedule for the week, especially with Evan's soccer practice and my client meetings. Also, I updated our emergency contact info just in case.

**Tuesday:**
- **Breakfast:** I’ll have the usual oatmeal; might try that new granola we picked up last weekend.
- **Lunch:** Planning to grab a salad at the coworking space on Burnet Road.
- **Dinner:** As you’re taking Evan to soccer, I’ll stay back with Laura. Maybe we can order from that new Thai place we’ve been wanting to try?
- **Tour:** Don’t forget, the school tour is scheduled for 10 a.m. I’ll handle drop-offs so you can head straight there.

**Wednesday:**
- **Breakfast:** Same as Tuesday, nothing too heavy since I have a long day ahead.
- **Lunch:** I’ll be at my desk, probably just a quick sandwich.
- **Dinner:** I have a late call with a client on the West Coast, so I’ll skip dinner with you guys. Make sure you and the kids have something easy.
- **Tour:** No tours scheduled for today, so that’s one less thing to juggle!

Regarding emergency contacts, I’ve ensured you’re listed with the new number: (512) 555-2198 and your email. It’s all up-to-date now.

Let me know if there's anything you need me to adjust or if there's anything specific we should discuss with Bob and Janet's next visit. I’m looking forward to a quiet weekend after this busy midweek hustle. Maybe we can finally plan that family trip to Colorado we’ve been talking about.

Talk soon,
Daniel

P.S. I’ll be working primarily from home, but if you need to reach me, give me a call at (512) 739-8842. Our zip code is still 78731, in case we need to update any delivery services.

-- 
Daniel R. Whitaker
Founder & Principal Data Consultant
Whitaker Analytics Group, LLC
Email: daniel.whitaker@whitakeranalytics.com
Phone: (512) 739-8842
